card.title: Why AI Confidently Makes Things Up
card.teaser: Language models don't know what they don't know — and that's not a bug, it's the whole design.
card.coverImagePrompt: A chatbot avatar confidently lecturing from a podium while books behind it are shown to be blank-paged illusions, soft spotlight, slightly eerie atmosphere, cinematic painterly

lessons.title: The lawyer who trusted ChatGPT
lessons.body: In June 2023, a New York attorney named Steven Schwartz filed a legal brief full of case citations. One problem: six of them didn't exist. ChatGPT had invented them — complete with plausible court names, judge initials, and docket numbers. The judge fined Schwartz $5,000. The cases were polished, confident, and completely fictional. So why did the machine lie?

visual.imagePrompt: An empty courtroom with legal books opening to reveal blank pages, dust motes catching light from a tall window

lessons.title: A model of the next word, not the world
lessons.body: Large language models don't store facts. They store statistics about which words follow which other words. When you ask for a 'famous 1923 physics paper,' the model produces the most plausible-sounding sequence of tokens given its training — not a database lookup. 'Plausible' and 'true' usually overlap. Sometimes they don't.

keyTerms.definition: A chunk of text (often a word fragment) that a language model reads and writes one at a time
The core task: given what's come before, pick the most likely next token

visual.imagePrompt: Probability clouds hovering over each word in a sentence, showing branching possibilities for the next word

lessons.title: Confidence is a side effect
lessons.body: A model is trained to sound like a fluent writer. Fluent writers sound sure. Nothing in that training rewards saying 'I don't know' — in fact, training data is full of confident assertions, so the model mimics them. This is why a hallucination reads just as crisp as a fact. The tone carries no signal about truth.
lessons.funFact: OpenAI's GPT-4 technical report showed that newer, larger models hallucinate less — but they also become harder to catch, because their mistakes sound more expert.

visual.imagePrompt: Two identical sound waves labeled 'true' and 'false' — indistinguishable

lessons.title: You can literally measure it
lessons.body: Researchers test hallucination rates by asking a model questions with known answers. In 2023, one Stanford benchmark (HaluEval) found that GPT-4 produced unsupported claims in roughly 19% of long-form answers. Smaller open-source models were often above 40%. The number keeps dropping with each model generation, but it never hits zero — because prediction isn't retrieval.

visual.imagePrompt: A dashboard with a dial labeled 'hallucination rate' hovering between 10% and 50%, warning lights blinking softly

lessons.title: The fix is architectural, not moral
lessons.body: You can't just tell a model to 'stop making things up' — it doesn't know which of its outputs are made up. The real fixes are engineering: retrieval-augmented generation (let the model look things up), tool use (let it call a calculator), and grounding (cite the source). Used well, language models are brilliant assistants. Used blindly, they're confident liars. The user's job is to know which.

quiz.question: Why do language models hallucinate?
options: They have bugs in their code
They predict plausible next tokens rather than look up facts
They are deliberately programmed to lie
quiz.explanation: LLMs generate text based on statistical likelihood, not database retrieval — so plausibility can outrun accuracy.

quiz.question: What happened in the Mata v. Avianca case in 2023?
options: A lawyer was fined for citing fake cases ChatGPT invented
A judge ruled AI could not be cited
ChatGPT was banned from New York
quiz.explanation: Attorney Steven Schwartz submitted a brief with six fictional cases generated by ChatGPT, and was fined $5,000.

quiz.question: Which of these helps reduce hallucinations in practice?
options: Retrieval-augmented generation (letting the model look things up)
Running the model at a hotter temperature
quiz.explanation: RAG grounds answers in real retrieved documents, which is one of the most effective architectural fixes.

sources.title: Mata v. Avianca, Inc., 22-cv-1461
sources.publisher: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York

sources.title: HaluEval: A Large-Scale Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for LLMs
